core: The 20-ppg 2024 season is the truest signal in the data; 2025's dip to 15.8 came with the elite weeks intact — a third of his starts still broke slates. Bell-cow usage in an offense built around him.
Back-to-back 19+ ppg seasons and an 85% hit rate — the rare back who's both explosive and dependable. Detroit's committee talk never actually capped him.
Three straight seasons of ~23 ppg — the rushing floor makes his bad days everyone else's good days. Age 30 is the only argument anyone has left against him.
The workload finally matched the talent: 19.7 ppg with elite games in 35% of starts, climbing three straight years. A new coordinator is the lone variable in an otherwise settled bell-cow picture.
Quietly climbed to 19.5 ppg while missing in only 15% of starts — target-hog usage with almost no empty weeks. The production keeps rising each year he's in this offense.
The breakout is real and steep — 7.0 to 11.9 to 17.8 ppg with a 94 opportunity score, the heaviest target load in the sport. New coordinator inherits a passing game that runs through him.
The floor merchant's floor merchant — three straight years in the 15-17 ppg band on relentless volume. What he lacks in spike weeks he repays in never losing you one.
Jumped from 14.2 to 20.8 ppg in year two, though only 7% of starts hit elite — the production is volume-steady, not ceiling-driven yet. The arrow points one direction.
The 9.4-ppg 2025 is the worst season of his career and screams situation, not skill — the 92 opportunity score says the targets never left. At 27, the résumé argues the cliff is a mirage.
A 91% hit rate and 18.6 ppg — tight end production that behaves like a WR1's. Three straight years of growth; the new coordinator is inheriting the league's most reliable weapon at the position.
Missed five games and still profiled like a top-tier option — 28% elite weeks at 23. The 2025 injury interrupts the story; it doesn't change it.
The rookie year was volume without polish — 12.9 ppg but busts in nearly half his starts. Everything rides on whether year two smooths the weekly variance, because the workload is already his.

Only 8 games in 2025, and the durability file is now thick — but the 22.5-ppg 2024 shows what a full season still looks like. A per-game elite with an availability discount baked into every conversation.
Sliding from 19.9 to 14.2 to 12.6 ppg across three seasons — the trend is the concern, not the talent. The Dallas passing volume that made him a monster hasn't been there since 2023.
Climbed to 18.7 ppg in year two, though 44% of starts still missed — the weekly variance is the last unpolished edge. The rushing contribution keeps the floor from ever fully collapsing.

The 16.8-ppg 2025 in an injury-shortened 13 games sits far below the 25.8 masterpiece before it. A new coordinator arrives with the question every 28-year-old rushing QB eventually faces: how much of the legs survive.
Rebounded to 20.1 ppg — his best per-game mark in three years — even as 37% of starts missed. The arm talent hasn't gone anywhere; the weekly consistency of the dynasty years has.
Three seasons between 15 and 18 ppg on a snap diet that never became a true bell-cow load. A new coordinator in Miami decides whether the efficiency finally meets full volume.
Seven games in 2025 cut the sample short, but a quarter of his career starts have hit elite — dual-threat production that travels. The injury year is noise until proven otherwise.

Climbed back to 20 ppg with elite games in a third of his starts — the 89 opportunity score says Indianapolis has re-committed completely. At 27, he's producing like the age curve hasn't read his file yet.
Nine rookie games at 13.3 ppg on a first-rounder's workload before the season was cut short. Small sample, heavy usage, new coordinator — the profile is all runway and unknowns.
When he plays, the targets are massive — an 87 opportunity score — but 45% of starts missed and he's lost chunks of two seasons. The talent-to-output gap is the entire evaluation.
Three seasons pinned between 16.7 and 18.1 ppg — high-floor, low-spike production that a new coordinator will try to unlock. The arm has always promised more weeks than the offense delivers.
Three straight years of growth to 17.2 ppg with a rising share of Buffalo's backfield. Approaching 27 with tread building — the production window and the age window are now the same conversation.
The 20.1-ppg 2025 is the season the draft pedigree always promised, though only 7% of starts hit elite — it was accumulation, not eruption. Consistency arrived before the ceiling did.
The move to Dallas unlocked a career-best 14.3 ppg on an 86 opportunity score, but 42% of starts still missed. Boom-bust wiring on a genuine alpha target share.
An 88 opportunity score as a rookie — Carolina force-fed him immediately — but 10.5 ppg and a 47% miss rate show the efficiency hasn't caught the usage. The role is secured; the polish isn't.
Four games in 2025 before the injury ended it, after a 14.5-ppg rookie season built on pure target gravity. At 22, the résumé is short, loud, and interrupted — with a new coordinator waiting.
A rookie tight end asked to matter immediately — 10.3 ppg with 19% elite weeks but misses in over half his starts. The flashes at 22 are the argument; the variance is the cost.
Nine games in 2025 at a career-best 19.9 ppg — efficient as ever when upright. Three seasons of near-identical per-game output make him the most stable QB profile nobody calls elite.
Back-to-back seasons near 18 ppg to start a career — steady, unspectacular, and durable. The 35% miss rate is the gap between good starter and weekly difference-maker.
The 19.2-ppg 2025 is his lowest in three years, and now a new coordinator inherits the league's most rushing-dependent QB profile at 28. The floor is still elite; the direction is the question.
The #3 pick lands in a backfield Arizona just cleared for him — no NFL snaps yet, but the draft capital says the plan is a full workload from day one.
A rookie tight end who got real usage — 11.1 ppg across a full 17 games. The position's learning curve says year-one volume like this usually precedes the year-two leap.
Slipped to 12.7 ppg after consecutive ~14.7 seasons, with a third of starts missing — the elite-week rate never matched the talent narrative. Still commands a top-tier target share at 27.
Fourteen rookie starts at 17.4 ppg — genuinely productive from the jump, with a new coordinator arriving to build around it. Short résumé, but nothing in it reads backup.
Rebounded to a career-best 13.6 ppg on an 88 opportunity score after the concussion-wrecked 2024. The route-running was never the question — availability and the offense around him were.
Eight games, 15.2 ppg, and elite weeks in a third of his career starts — per-snap production that rivals anyone's. The file is all interruptions: what exists of it is outstanding.
Two seasons stuck in the 15s after the 18.9 breakout, with only 7% of starts hitting elite — the 2023 ceiling hasn't reappeared. The talent-flash-to-steady-output conversion is still incomplete.
Two straight seasons around 14.5 ppg as Cincinnati's clear lead back — unspectacular weekly output on very spectacular volume. The role, not the ceiling, is what he offers.
Was breaking out — 14.7 ppg through 8 games — before the injury stopped it. Three straight years of growth at tight end, where late-blooming arcs like this are the position's signature.
Bounced back to 18.3 ppg over a full 17 at age 32 — still a weekly starter, now with the shortest runway among top QB assets. Every year from here is a lease, not a purchase.
The Kansas City move follows his worst season — 10.4 ppg with a 43% miss rate and a shrinking role. The vision and burst that made him are intact; the situation that suppressed him just changed.
The rookie year split the difference — 18% elite weeks against misses in half his starts, classic explosive-back-in-a-committee output. The flashes are real; the workload isn't yet.
Three straight years of decline from 20.1 to 14.7 ppg, but an 82% hit rate — the volume is eroding while the reliability isn't. A floor back whose ceiling left first.
The rookie year barely happened — 7 ppg and misses in 80% of starts buried in a crowded room. Second-round pedigree at 22 buys patience the production hasn't earned yet.
New England buys the target monopoly he never quite had in Philadelphia — though the 12.1-ppg 2025 was his quietest year since his rookie season. At 29, the physicality profile ages better than most.

The 21.5-ppg age-30 season on a 99 opportunity score is one of the great late-career workloads ever recorded — sandwiched around a lost 2024. Nobody's ceiling is higher; nobody's actuarial table is worse.
Two straight seasons nearer 10 ppg than 15, with misses in nearly two-thirds of starts — Denver is betting the Miami role, not the player, was the problem. The speed never left the profile.

Ticked up to 12.1 ppg but missed in exactly half his starts — heavy usage in a run-first ecosystem that caps receiver spikes. A new coordinator is the most interesting thing to happen to his target quality.

Three straight seasons of decline from 14.7 to 12.1 ppg, with misses in 44% of starts — the explosion never translated into weekly trust. A new coordinator gets one more shot at the version everyone drafted.
Three seasons parked in the 17-19 ppg band — metronome production with a 9% elite rate, at 31. What you see is precisely, reliably, unspectacularly what you get.
Seven games in 2025 and three straight years stuck near 11 ppg — elite separation skills that Jets quarterbacking has never once paid off. The new coordinator is the latest test of the thesis.
A 79 opportunity score as a rookie — Cleveland handed him the job — but 11.1 ppg with misses in 57% of starts. The workload arrived before the efficiency did.
Year two nudged up to 10.3 ppg, still missing in 69% of starts — first-round pedigree producing like a WR4 in a crowded room. The DJ Moore departure clears his best path yet.
An 18% elite rate as a rookie shows the juice, but 9.5 ppg and a 65% miss rate show the queue in Tampa. Evans's exit and a new coordinator reshuffle everything above him.
The 15.6-ppg 2024 proved the ceiling; the 12-ppg 2025 re-proved the pattern — health and Chase's gravity decide his output more than his talent does. A 19% elite rate says the spike weeks still live.
A 70% hit rate across two seasons — quietly one of the most dependable young backs in the league — cut short at 10 games in 2025. The new coordinator inherits a backfield that already worked.
The 13.8-ppg rookie season keeps receding — 10.9 then 11.9 on just 9 games — but the pedigree and the Detroit offense keep the case alive. The 60 opportunity score is the quiet worry.
A 22% elite rate on 11 ppg — the league's purest boom-bust receiver, hitting slate-breakers between empty weeks. Speed that has never learned a floor.

The 20.9-ppg 2024 masterpiece gave way to 13.4 at age 29 with a new coordinator arriving — the exact arc the age curve predicts for backs coming off 400-touch seasons. A 25% elite rate says the big weeks haven't left yet.
The 12.4-ppg 2025 was finally his best year since the rookie explosion — half a decade of waiting for a payoff that arrived at a whisper. A new coordinator is either the unlock or the last excuse.
The rookie year was rough by any measure — 11.8 ppg, zero elite weeks, misses in three-quarters of starts. The #1 pick pedigree buys the runway; nothing in year one shortened it.
The 13.9-ppg rookie breakout collapsed to 8.2 in an injury-marred year two — fourteen games that muddied a trajectory that looked ballistic. The talent case rests entirely on 2024.
The 18.8-ppg rookie brilliance has faded to 14.9 with a 3% elite rate — two straight seasons where the magic never re-materialized. The passing talent is obvious; the fantasy output isn't.
Five games in Minnesota after the trade, with three prior seasons of slow drift from 18 to 16 ppg. A 28-year-old reset button in a new system — the rushing floor is the one constant.
The 12.4-ppg rookie year fell to 9.2 as defenses adjusted and the offense contracted. A new coordinator and the Keenan Allen vacuum give the route-running savant his cleanest runway yet.
Eleven starts at 14.5 ppg with a 64% hit rate and zero elite weeks — competent immediately, spectacular never. A 26-year-old rookie's window runs on a faster clock.
The 14.3-ppg 2025 on an 87 opportunity score is his best football since the knee injury — Dallas fed him and he held up. Two lost years in the middle make the true baseline hard to price.
Slipped to 9.6 ppg in an offense that stopped throwing — the 53% hit rate is the residue of target scarcity, not skill decay. The Brown departure and a new coordinator redraw his entire target map.
A 75% hit rate as a rookie tight end — 11.8 ppg at 21 with real weekly dependability, which almost never happens at the position that young. The profile is quietly exceptional.
An 88% hit rate — the most dependable week-to-week back in football — on three straight quality seasons. At 28, every remaining year is productive tread on a depreciating asset.
Top-5 draft capital dropped into a Titans receiver room with targets to spare — no snaps yet, but the path to immediate WR1 usage is unobstructed.
Top-10 capital in New Orleans, where the departures ahead of him cleared a runway most rookie receivers wait years for.
Rebounded to 13.9 ppg on an 86 opportunity score after the lost 2024, now carrying the New Orleans backfield. A 50% miss rate says the volume is steadier than the output.
The 21.6-ppg 2024 spike settled back to 16.1 — the career pattern is competence punctuated by one glorious outlier. At 31 with a new coordinator, you're buying the median, not the memory.
Eight rookie games at 14.5 ppg with a 75% hit rate before injury ended it — bruising, dependable, immediately useful. The sample is small; nothing in it is soft.
First-round capital in Las Vegas with the depth chart cleared for a rookie audition — the profile is entirely projection, and the landing spot is entirely opportunity.

Two years, 9.9 then 8.8 ppg — the most decorated receiver prospect in a decade producing like a WR4. Nothing in the pedigree explains it, which is exactly why the debate rages; a new coordinator is the next variable.
The 18.6-ppg Minnesota revival cooled to 14.3 in Seattle — the career-long pattern of environment-dependence reasserting itself. A new coordinator makes him a system bet, again.
A 46% elite rate — nearly half his starts break slates, the highest big-week frequency at the position — at age 32 on 11 games. The ceiling never aged; the calendar did.
The Indianapolis reinvention is real: 17.7 ppg, a career best, on 13 starts. Three years of steady climb since the Giants wreckage — the league's most improbable ascending QB profile.
Three straight years of growth to 10.7 ppg with a 16% elite rate — the deep-ball specialist slowly becoming a complete receiver. The trajectory is modest but has never once reversed.
A 21-ppg season at age 38 — the best per-game year of his Rams tenure, defying every actuarial table. Each additional season is found money on an expiring lease.
Back to 11.5 ppg in 10 games after the lost 2024 — the speed and size still flash, the availability still doesn't. A 68% career miss rate is the price of the highlight reel.
Two straight years around 14 ppg — the rebuild from the benching is real but shallow, with a 60% miss rate still haunting the weekly output. The arrow points up from a very low floor.
Rookie capital in a Seattle backfield with a new coordinator and no entrenched bell-cow — a profile that's all situation, no sample.
Rebounded to 11.2 ppg over a full 17 — the reliable middle of the tight end pack, elevated by Dallas passing volume. The 10% elite rate caps what the role can pay.
Ticked up to 13.5 ppg — his best output in three years — while never shaking the 45% miss rate. A career of good-not-great weeks in perfect fantasy-friendly usage.
Three seasons in, still waiting: 10.5 ppg is a new high, but the 4% elite rate and 62 opportunity score say Buffalo has never actually featured him. The pedigree case gets thinner each year.

An 88% hit rate and 16.4 ppg at age 32 — the workload science says this shouldn't exist, and it keeps existing. Every week is borrowed time performed magnificently; a new coordinator inherits the anomaly.
Nine games at 7.8 ppg in year two — the flashes are real, the availability and the crowded 49ers pecking order keep burying them. Still more theory than track record.
A full 17-game rookie season at 10.9 ppg — durable and involved, but a 53% miss rate and modest role kept the ceiling theoretical. Volume tolerance proven; explosiveness pending.
Fell to 8.2 ppg in the Minnesota chaos — the QB carousel took his target quality with it. Two competent years before that argue the profile is a passenger of the situation.
A rookie parked behind Stafford in Los Angeles — the profile is a lottery ticket on the succession, nothing more yet.
Top-10 capital dropped into the target vacuum A.J. Brown left behind — no snaps yet, but Philadelphia drafted him to inherit a featured role immediately.
A full rookie season of 6.4 ppg with zero elite weeks — Houston gave him snaps, not targets. The second-round pedigree awaits an actual role.
Seventeen rookie games at 7.9 ppg with no elite weeks — a grinder's usage in a backfield that never committed. Involvement without emphasis.
Three straight years of quiet growth to 9 ppg — the depth receiver slowly making himself un-cuttable. The 73% miss rate says the fantasy case is still mostly patience.
Three straight years of decline to 8.7 ppg, now in Buffalo betting a QB upgrade reverses the slide at 29. The 15% elite rate says the big-play gene survived the down years.
A rookie year of 5.7 ppg and misses in 87% of starts — but Etienne's departure just made him the presumptive lead in Jacksonville. The opportunity spike is the whole story.
First-round capital, 4 ppg, and a 93% miss rate — a rookie year that produced almost nothing but changed almost nothing about the draft-day thesis. Year two is the real evaluation.
Ten games at 9.5 ppg at age 30, with a new coordinator arriving — the years of elite work against terrible quarterbacking may have inverted: now the situation is fine and the clock is the problem.
The streak machine finally cracked — 8 games, 8.7 ppg at 32, now in San Francisco. A 23% elite rate even in decline says the red-zone gravity is the last thing to go.
Seven games at 7.8 ppg — two straight injury-shortened, declining seasons after the promising debut. The slot skills are real; the durability record is now the headline.
Cratered to 9.1 ppg at 29 in the move to Houston — the dependable TD-bank years in Detroit look increasingly like the peak. A 61% hit rate is what remains of the floor.
Three seasons oscillating around 11 ppg on a genuine alpha target share — an 83 opportunity score at age 30. Denver keeps feeding him; the output stays stubbornly mid.
Four Miami starts at 13.3 ppg — the running ability keeps producing whenever someone lets him play. Still a profile of flashes auditioning for a job that's never been his.
Fell to 6.4 ppg in year two — the speed never translated into a reliable role in a Chiefs offense that spreads everyone thin. A 71% miss rate on elite athleticism is the puzzle.
Career-best 10.7 ppg on an 82 opportunity score, now in Tennessee with a new coordinator — the target-hog slot role travels with him. Volume is his entire fantasy identity.
Rookie capital in Cleveland's rebuilt receiver room — no snaps yet, and a depth chart fluid enough that the path is real.
Fifteen rookie games at 8.9 ppg with a 13% elite rate — genuine flashes at a position where rookies rarely flash. The new coordinator decides how fast the role grows.
Early draft capital at tight end in New York — nothing on tape yet, at the position with the league's slowest onboarding.
Fell to 6.8 ppg after the promising 10.5 season — the slot role that looked ascendant got squeezed. The quickness is intact; the target share went elsewhere.
Rookie back in a Denver committee — the profile is draft capital and patience, with Harvey and Dobbins ahead of the usage.
Collapsed from the 14.9-ppg breakout to 7.5 — the Carolina backfield re-crowded and his role evaporated with it. The 2024 tape argues he's better than the 2025 usage.
Three seasons of gentle decline to 10.5 ppg, now 28 with a new coordinator in Pittsburgh. The physical tools never dulled; the target quality around him did.
Two years buried — 7 ppg with an 84% miss rate behind Kyren Williams. The draft pedigree keeps him relevant; the depth chart keeps him theoretical.
Seven games, 7.1 ppg, and a 14% elite rate amid the two-way experiment — the most unique evaluation in football remains unevaluated. The talent is generational; the sample is a shrug.
Three straight years of growth to 9.8 ppg — the quiet ascent of a tight end learning a featured role. Modest numbers, uninterrupted trajectory.
Still commanding an 89 opportunity score at nearly 34 — 13.8 ppg with a 21% elite rate, defying the position's aging script. Every remaining season is a gift with a receipt.
Three identical ~8.5-ppg seasons, now in New England — the definition of a known quantity. A steady WR3 profile that has never once threatened to be more.
Career-best 12.3 ppg with the backfield finally tilting his way — then Pittsburgh imported Rico Dowdle and a new coordinator. The efficiency was never the question; the commitment is.
Three straight years of growth to 11.4 ppg — the bust label aged badly. Keenan Allen's exit and a new coordinator hand him the biggest target opportunity of his career.
Nine starts at 13.6 ppg with zero elite weeks — steady, cautious, unspectacular. A new coordinator inherits a first-rounder still auditioning for conviction.
Ten starts at 12.7 ppg in his delayed debut — a 50/50 hit rate and no elite weeks, but every rep is the first data anyone has. The evaluation genuinely starts now.
Two straight declining seasons to 7.5 ppg — the knee injury marked a before-and-after the production never recovered from. At 29, the résumé outweighs the recent tape.
Ten games at 11 ppg — another season, another partial sample from the most talented back who can't stay on the field. The per-game output has never been the problem.
Three seasons within a point of 11 ppg — dependable, unspectacular volume in an offense that keeps him busy but never featured. The definition of replaceable production.
A rookie tight end landing in the Eagles' reshuffled passing game — no snaps yet, at the position where patience is mandatory.
Cratered to 7.7 ppg at age 30 with Baltimore's offense migrating away from him — three straight years of decline from the 13.5 peak. A new coordinator is more threat than promise at this point.
A career-best 10.7 ppg over a full 17 — the steady possession role in Arizona keeps growing. Modest ceiling, quietly rising floor, new coordinator incoming.
Career-best 10.8 ppg over a full season at 29 — the late-career TE consolidation year that the position occasionally produces. The 3% elite rate defines the ceiling.
Settled at 8.3 ppg with zero elite weeks — Buffalo's most trustworthy and least explosive target. The role is secure; the fantasy ceiling was never part of the deal.
Still a 70% hit rate and 21% elite weeks at age 36 — the decline is real, from an altitude nobody else at the position has ever occupied. Each season is a farewell tour with production.
Career-best 10.6 ppg with the Walker trade finally clearing the Seattle backfield — and a new coordinator to re-deal the touches. The opportunity just arrived; the résumé says he'll hold it.
Rookie capital in a Jets receiver room mid-teardown — no snaps, fluid depth chart, everything to prove.
Two straight sub-10-ppg years, now in Pittsburgh with a new coordinator at 28 — the alpha-volume Indianapolis seasons keep receding. A possession profile in search of a featured role.
Dropped to 11.5 ppg and lost the Miami job — Atlanta is the accuracy-over-arm thesis's last venue. The concussion history shadows every projection.
A full 17-game rookie season at 8.2 ppg in a Washington committee — durable, involved, unremarkable. The new coordinator re-opens the role competition.
Eight games, 5.2 ppg, zero hits — three years post-draft and the knee has still never let the evaluation happen. All pedigree, no proof.
Two fringe seasons around 7 ppg — the undrafted success story that stalled at the roster-spot stage. The 73% miss rate is the fantasy translation.
Back-to-back 11+ ppg seasons earned him the Pittsburgh deal — a late-blooming grinder whose new coordinator imported him on purpose. Unspectacular and suddenly relevant.
Fell to 5.3 ppg in the move to New York — the Andrews-handcuff hype never survived contact with a real target tree. The athletic flashes remain a memory, not a trend.
Slipped to 8.8 ppg at nearly 30 — a decade of quiet competence entering its budget-cut years. The hands never failed; the situation and the calendar are doing the failing.
Three seasons flat at ~7 ppg, now in Washington with a new coordinator — athletic testing that has never once become a featured role. The profile is perpetual potential.
Rookie capital in Cleveland's remade receiver room — no snaps yet; the depth chart is soft enough that camp decides everything.
Back to 10.5 ppg over a full 17 at age 30 — the steady veteran floor at a position starved for it. Nothing about the profile surprises in either direction.
A quiet 10.5 ppg at nearly 32 — the reliable target New England keeps re-signing. Every year is the last year, and every year he posts the same line.
Rookie capital in a Tennessee backfield with a new coordinator and no committed starter — the path is cleaner than the pedigree is loud.
The 13.2-ppg 2024 outlier collapsed back to 5 — the career-long pattern of one great year per team reasserting itself. At nearly 31, the market has stopped waiting.
Grew to 8.7 ppg in year two — quietly useful in an offense that just lost pieces above him. A new coordinator and a thinned target tree are his opening.
A career-best 11 ppg at 27 in the Tampa committee — the perpetual backup finally getting real touches. The new coordinator decides whether the audition continues.
Rookie capital in a Washington receiver room with a new coordinator and no settled hierarchy — path over pedigree.
Collapsed from 14.1 to 7.2 ppg in two years, now on his second team — the receiving-back skill set intact, the featured role gone. A change of scenery bet at 27.

Two straight ~9.8-ppg seasons of honest committee work — and the Giants just spent a pick on Cam Skattebo for the same backfield. Dependable depth, now with real competition for the touches he'd earned.
A rookie year of 3.4 ppg and zero hits despite second-round capital — buried and inefficient. The new coordinator restart is the whole remaining case.
Three seasons of slow decline to 10.3 ppg at 29 — the burst that defined the Dallas years shows up less each season. Volume is holding the profile together.
Fell back to 7.7 ppg in the Minnesota committee — the 2024 flash never became a role. Efficient enough to keep, never trusted enough to feature.
A 94% miss rate across two post-injury seasons — the electric rookie burst has not reappeared. The Chargers move is a flyer on the memory of speed.
Grew to 7.5 ppg in a grinder's role, now in the Etienne-vacated Jacksonville backfield — a power back whose opportunity finally outpaces his pedigree.
The 16.1-ppg 2024 collapsed to 7.2 across 9 injury-limited games at 30 — the ankle changed everything. A new coordinator inherits either a rebound or a retirement arc.
Four games in 2025 stalled the sophomore evaluation entirely — the rookie flashes remain the only real data. Tampa's reshuffled receiver room decides his re-entry point.
Rookie capital in Pittsburgh with a new coordinator — no snaps, no verdict, just a depth chart worth watching.
Eight starts at 10.9 ppg with misses in nearly 90% of them — the debut was loud in coverage and quiet in production. A 13% elite rate hints the arm plays; nothing else has yet.
A rookie back in Kansas City's perpetually ambiguous backfield — no snaps yet, in the offense that launders running back value.
Three sub-7-ppg seasons of honest backup work, now traded into Arizona's rebuilt backfield — except the #3 pick arrived in the same offseason. The workload math is unforgiving.
Three straight declining years to 9.9 ppg at nearly 33, currently unsigned — the route precision endures while the explosion and the market don't. A landing spot away from one last relevant season.
Zero games in 2025 after the knee, unsigned at 28 — two years removed from the 13.4-ppg peak. The profile is entirely a medical report now.
Rookie capital in Miami's remodeled receiver room — no snaps, new coordinator, wide-open target tree.
A rookie year of 4.9 ppg and an 87% miss rate in the Cleveland committee — the speed flashed; the role never came. Year two is about touches, not talent.
Two straight collapsing seasons to 6 ppg, now in Detroit's crowded backfield — the violent running style that made him also wore him down. The Kansas City peak feels a long time ago.
Still starting at 42, still around 14 ppg — the twilight is real but astonishingly slow. Every additional week is sports history; none of it is dynasty value.
Twelve games at 8.9 ppg at age 31 — the reliability that defined him is now the thing in decline. A 66% hit rate is the last asset standing.
A rookie behind Murray in Arizona with a new coordinator — the profile is a clipboard and a what-if.
Fell to 6.5 ppg in the Seattle move — the deep-ball specialist's output has always been quarterback-dependent, and the new coordinator resets the equation again.
Rookie capital in Baltimore's run-first ecosystem — no snaps yet, in the offense that historically starves receiver depth.
Dropped to 11.6 ppg at 35 in the Jets reunion — the improbable renaissance is decelerating on schedule. A bridge quarterback in every sense.
Three seasons stuck around 7-8 ppg — the receiving-back skill set that never escaped the committee. A new coordinator is the next chance to matter.
Rookie capital in the Giants' receiver room with a new coordinator — no snaps, wide-open depth chart, everything speculative.
Zero games in 2025, three years since resembling a starter — the contract is the only reason the conversation continues. A new coordinator inherits the roster spot, not a plan.
A 16.7-ppg season at 33 — the career backup's best per-game year, produced exactly when Arizona needed a bridge. Competence for rent, never for keeps.
Fell to 3.5 ppg in the Philadelphia move — the power flashes of 2024 disappeared into a stacked backfield. A change of role, not scenery, is what the profile needs.
A rookie back in Minnesota's committee — no snaps yet, no obstruction that couldn't be run through either.
Fell to 7.8 ppg at 30 in the Chargers move — the athletic peak years produced exactly one great season. A 17% elite rate says the spike weeks still occasionally arrive.
Resurfaced at 7.2 ppg in Miami after the Denver washout — the seam speed that made him a pick is still visible in flashes. Waller's exit and a new coordinator open one more door.
A depth receiver in Buffalo with no snaps of consequence — roster-spot territory, watchlist at most.
Two years, 4.6 ppg peak, a 91% miss rate — elite testing that has produced almost nothing. The Jets and a new coordinator are the second chance the tools keep buying.
A rookie year that never started — 0.8 ppg, zero hits, buried — and then Pittsburgh imported Dowdle ahead of him. The draft capital is the only bull case left standing.
Rookie capital in Tampa's reshuffled receiver room — no snaps; the Evans departure is the reason to keep the name filed.
An undrafted-profile back in the 49ers system — no production yet; the system's history of manufacturing value is the entire appeal.
Collapsed to 8 ppg at age 30 with Etienne now imported into his backfield — the twilight arrived on schedule. The receiving craft is the last durable skill.
A rookie year of 2.3 ppg and zero hits despite day-two capital — the Raiders offense buried everyone. New coordinator, clean slate, unproven everything.
Rookie capital in Atlanta with a new coordinator — no snaps yet; the return-game electricity is the placeholder skill.
Career-high 5 ppg in a full season — depth-receiver output on depth-receiver usage, now in Las Vegas. The speed is the résumé.
A no-snap rookie in Carolina's receiver room — filed under name-to-know, nothing more yet.
A rookie tight end in the Rams pipeline — no production, at the position where year one is always silent.
Cratered to 5.7 ppg in the post-trade wreckage — one 11.3-ppg season in six years is the actual track record. The route talent has outlived three offenses' attempts to use it.
Grew to 8.5 ppg in year two — the late-round grinder quietly out-producing his draft slot. A new coordinator reshuffles a backfield he's been climbing.
A rookie tight end in New Orleans — no snaps, long runway, standard-issue patience required.
